findings.title: Large circular dependency web: 15 files
findings.description:
**Large dependency web:** 15 interconnected files form a strongly connected component.

**Files involved (showing 8/15):**
  • `file_revision.rs`
  • `files.rs`
  • `file_root.rs`
  • `path.rs`
  • `system.rs`
  • `memory_fs.rs`
  • `os.rs`
  • `test.rs`
  ...and 7 more

**Entry points (imported from outside the cycle):**
  • `os.rs` (imported 126 times)
  • `session.rs` (imported 75 times)
  • `test.rs` (imported 62 times)
  • `diagnostic.rs` (imported 25 times)
  • `system.rs` (imported 12 times)

**Weakest link to break:** `file_revision.rs` → `files.rs` (1 import)
findings.suggested_fix:
🔗 **Best place to break:** `file_revision.rs` → `files.rs` (weakest coupling: 1 imports)

Since `file_revision.rs` only imports 1 symbol(s) from `files.rs`, consider:
- Move those symbols to a shared module
- Pass them as parameters instead of importing
- Use dependency injection

**For large cycles:**
1. Extract shared interfaces/types into a new `types.py` or `interfaces.ts`
2. Apply the Dependency Inversion Principle
3. Consider restructuring into layers
findings.why_it_matters: Circular dependencies make code harder to understand, test, and maintain. They can cause import errors at runtime and make it difficult to refactor individual modules.

findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
📦 In function: `copy_file` (1 callers)
🎯 User input detected in context
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: Architectural Bottleneck: bindings (architectural hub)
findings.description:
Function 'bindings' is called by 30 functions and has complexity 44. Changes here are high-risk.

Betweenness centrality: 0.1344 - this function sits on many execution paths between other functions.

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 44 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=7, p95=12, mean=3.5, n=21613
findings.suggested_fix: This is a critical hub. Ensure comprehensive test coverage, add defensive error handling, and consider circuit breaker pattern.
findings.why_it_matters: Bottlenecks are single points of failure that amplify bugs. High fan-in with high complexity means many callers depend on risky code.

findings.title: Test code in production: 3 issues
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Test fixtures (lines 12, 28, 42)
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
📦 In function: `hover` (4 callers)
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: Potential infinite loop
findings.description:
Loop with no apparent exit condition detected.

**Analysis:**
⚠️ No break/return found in loop body
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:
1. Add a break condition
2. Add a return statement
3. If intentional, add a comment: # Intentional infinite loop

Example:
```python
while True:
data = get_data()
if data is None:
break  # Exit condition
process(data)
```
findings.why_it_matters: Infinite loops without exit conditions will hang the program and consume 100% CPU. Even intentional infinite loops (servers) should have shutdown mechanisms.

findings.title: Circular dependency: 5 files
findings.description:
Mutual dependency between: dunder_all.rs ↔ place.rs ↔ semantic_index.rs ↔ place.rs ↔ semantic_model.rs

**Weakest link to break:** `dunder_all.rs` → `place.rs` (1 import)
findings.suggested_fix:
🔗 **Best place to break:** `dunder_all.rs` → `place.rs` (weakest coupling: 1 imports)

Since `dunder_all.rs` only imports 1 symbol(s) from `place.rs`, consider:
- Move those symbols to a shared module
- Pass them as parameters instead of importing
- Use dependency injection

**For large cycles:**
1. Extract shared interfaces/types into a new `types.py` or `interfaces.ts`
2. Apply the Dependency Inversion Principle
3. Consider restructuring into layers
findings.why_it_matters: Circular dependencies make code harder to understand, test, and maintain. They can cause import errors at runtime and make it difficult to refactor individual modules.

findings.title: Hook called conditionally: `useCallback`
findings.description:
React hooks must be called in the exact same order on every render.

**Analysis:**
🪝 Hook: `useCallback`
📦 Component: `Chrome`
⚠️ Called inside `if/else/switch/ternary`
findings.why_it_matters:
This violates the Rules of Hooks. React relies on the order of hook calls to track state correctly. Conditional/loop/nested hooks cause:
• State getting out of sync
• Crashes and rendering bugs
• Unpredictable behavior

findings.title: Test code in production: 4 issues
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Mock/stub objects (lines 51, 93, 98, 103)
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: High panic density in `setup_tomllib_case`: 6 panic-family calls
findings.description: Function `setup_tomllib_case` contains 6 calls to `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `panic!()` at line 82, line 85, line 98, line 103, line 110, line 115. This makes the function fragile and likely to panic at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix:
Reduce panic points by:
- Using `?` operator for error propagation
- Using `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_default` / `unwrap_or_else`
- Returning `Result<T, E>` instead of panicking
- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.

findings.title: Regex compiled inside loop
findings.description:
Regex compiled in loop (loop starts line 180).

**Performance impact:** Regex compilation is expensive. In a loop of N iterations, this costs N compilations instead of 1.
findings.suggested_fix:
Move regex compilation outside the loop:
```rust
let re = Regex::new(pattern)?;  // Before loop
for item in items {
re.is_match(item);  // Reuse
}
```
findings.why_it_matters: Regex compilation involves parsing and optimization. Doing it once instead of N times can dramatically improve performance.

findings.title: String concatenation in loop
findings.description:
Variable 'fields_list' concatenated 2 times inside loop (started line 1013).

**Performance:** O(n²) time complexity. Each concatenation creates a new string and copies all previous characters.

For 1000 iterations, this copies ~500,000 characters instead of 1000.
findings.suggested_fix:
Use list and join:
```python
parts = []
for item in items:
parts.append(str(item))
result = ''.join(parts)
```
Or use a list comprehension:
```python
result = ''.join(str(item) for item in items)
```
findings.why_it_matters: String concatenation in loops creates O(n²) time complexity due to immutable string copying.

findings.title: Mutex poisoning risk
findings.description: Using .lock().unwrap() will panic if the mutex is poisoned. Consider handling the PoisonError or using parking_lot::Mutex.
findings.suggested_fix:
Handle poisoning: `mutex.lock().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner())`
Or use `parking_lot::Mutex` which has no poisoning.
findings.why_it_matters: Poisoned mutexes cause cascading panics across threads.

findings.title: Pandas chain indexing
findings.description: df['col1']['col2'] uses chain indexing, which can cause SettingWithCopyWarning and silent bugs when assigning values.
findings.suggested_fix:
Use .loc[] for explicit indexing:
```python
# Instead of:
df['col1']['col2'] = value  # Unreliable!

# Use:
df.loc[:, 'col1'] = value  # Single column
df.loc[mask, 'col'] = value  # With condition
```
findings.why_it_matters: Chain indexing returns a copy in some cases and a view in others. Assignments may silently fail, corrupting your data without warning. The SettingWithCopyWarning exists for this reason.

findings.description: == performs type coercion which can cause subtle bugs.
findings.why_it_matters:
Type coercion in == can cause unexpected behavior:
• '1' == 1 is true
• '' == false is true
• [] == false is true
Use === to compare both value AND type.
